comment_text.snp4.11:2.1: Piya, “things held dear” or “what is liked”.
comment_text.snp4.11:3.4: Samparāya means “in the next life”. The Niddesa’s gloss of “refuge, shelter” etc. does not change this but qualifies it: people look for safety in the next life.
comment_text.snp4.11:7.2: Here dvaya refers back to the “pair” of the previous lines and should not be overinterpreted as “duality”.
comment_text.snp4.11:12.1: The past participle sameta here is glossed by Niddesa as paṭipanna, i.e. “engaged in the practice”.
comment_text.snp4.11:13.1: Visaññī is used of one whose perception is distorted by grief (Thig 6.2:1.2), by alcohol (Bu Pc 51:1.40), or by the four “distortions of perception” (AN 4.49:3.5). Saññasaññī is the opposite.
comment_text.snp4.11:13.2: This verse answers the question of how one is to practice so that form disappears. It describes the process that leads from the fourth jhāna to the formless states.
comment_text.snp4.11:13.4: In MN 18, we have the sequence perception, thought, proliferation, then papañcasaññāsaṅkhā. This suggests that papañca causes saṅkhā, as per Bodhi.
comment_text.snp4.11:14.1: Oddly, however, the disappearance of sukha and dukkha is not directly answered. It is, however, implied in the surmounting of 3rd jhāna.
comment_text.snp4.11:14.4: While yakkha (Sanskrit yakṣa) is normally a worldly deity, he is identified with the ultimate Brahman at Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 5.4.1 and Kena Upaniṣad 4.1.
comment_text.snp4.11:15.1: At AN 10.29:20.2, the best of those who advocate the “ultimate purity of the spirit” are said to be those who teach the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, by which the Buddha is referring to the Brahmanical teacher Uddaka Rāmaputta (MN 26:16.13), said by the Buddha to be “astute” (paṇḍita MN 26:23.5).
comment_text.snp4.11:15.3: Niddesa treats samaya as “stilling” (sama), in the sense of the cessation of a sentient being. The normal meaning is “occasion”, which fits well here. Niddesa and commentary explain this second class of so-called experts as the annihilationists.

translation_text.snp4.11:1.1: “Where do quarrels and disputes come from?
translation_text.snp4.11:1.2: And lamentation and sorrow, and stinginess?
translation_text.snp4.11:1.3: What of conceit and arrogance, and slander too—
translation_text.snp4.11:1.4: tell me please, where do they come from?”
translation_text.snp4.11:2.1: “Quarrels and disputes come from what we hold dear,
translation_text.snp4.11:2.2: as do lamentation and sorrow, stinginess,
translation_text.snp4.11:2.4: Quarrels and disputes are linked to stinginess,
translation_text.snp4.11:2.5: and when disputes have arisen there is slander.”
translation_text.snp4.11:3.1: “So where do things held dear <j>in the world spring from?
translation_text.snp4.11:3.2: And the lusts that are loose in the world?
translation_text.snp4.11:3.3: Where spring the hopes and aims
translation_text.snp4.11:3.4: a man has for the next life?”
translation_text.snp4.11:4.1: “What we hold dear in the world spring from desire,
translation_text.snp4.11:4.2: as do the lusts that are loose in the world.
translation_text.snp4.11:4.3: From there spring the hopes and aims
translation_text.snp4.11:4.4: a man has for the next life.”
translation_text.snp4.11:5.1: “So where does desire in the world spring from?
translation_text.snp4.11:5.2: And evaluations, too, where do they come from?
translation_text.snp4.11:5.4: and other things spoken of by the Ascetic?”
translation_text.snp4.11:6.1: “What they call pleasure and pain in the world—
translation_text.snp4.11:6.2: based on that, desire comes about.
translation_text.snp4.11:6.3: Seeing the manifestation and vanishing of forms,
translation_text.snp4.11:6.4: a personage makes evaluations in the world.
translation_text.snp4.11:7.2: these things are, too, when that pair is present.
translation_text.snp4.11:7.3: One who is indecisive should train <j>in the path of knowledge;
translation_text.snp4.11:7.4: it is from knowledge <j>that the Ascetic speaks of these things.”
translation_text.snp4.11:8.1: “Where do pleasure and pain spring from?
translation_text.snp4.11:8.2: When what is absent do these things not occur?
translation_text.snp4.11:8.3: And also, on the topic of <j>manifesting and vanishing—
translation_text.snp4.11:8.4: tell me where they spring from.”
translation_text.snp4.11:9.2: when contact is absent they do not occur.
translation_text.snp4.11:9.3: And on the topic of <j>manifesting and vanishing—
translation_text.snp4.11:9.4: I tell you they spring from there.”
translation_text.snp4.11:10.1: “So where does contact in the world spring from?
translation_text.snp4.11:10.2: And possessions, too, where do they come from?
translation_text.snp4.11:10.3: When what is absent is there no possessiveness?
translation_text.snp4.11:10.4: When what vanishes do contacts not strike?”
translation_text.snp4.11:11.3: when wishing is absent there is no possessiveness;
translation_text.snp4.11:11.4: when form vanishes, contacts don’t strike.”
translation_text.snp4.11:12.2: And how do happiness and suffering vanish?
translation_text.snp4.11:12.4: I think we ought to know these things.”
translation_text.snp4.11:13.2: not lacking perception, nor perceiving what has vanished.
translation_text.snp4.11:13.3: Form vanishes for one proceeding thus;
translation_text.snp4.11:13.4: for judgments due to proliferation spring from perception.”
translation_text.snp4.11:14.1: “Whatever I asked you have explained to me.
translation_text.snp4.11:14.2: I ask you once more, please tell me this:
translation_text.snp4.11:14.3: Do some astute folk here say that this <j>is the highest extent
translation_text.snp4.11:14.5: Or do they say it is something else?”
translation_text.snp4.11:15.1: “Some astute folk do say that this <j>is the highest extent
translation_text.snp4.11:15.3: But some of them, claiming to be experts,
translation_text.snp4.11:15.4: speak of an occasion when there is no residue.
translation_text.snp4.11:16.1: Knowing that these states are dependent,
translation_text.snp4.11:16.2: and knowing what they depend on, <j>the inquiring sage,
translation_text.snp4.11:16.3: having understood, is freed, and enters no dispute.
translation_text.snp4.11:16.4: The attentive do not proceed to life after life.”

variant_text.snp4.11:12.4: jāniyāmāti → jānissāmāti (bj, mr); jāniyāma iti (sya-all, pts-vp-pli1)

reference_text.snp4.11:14.1: csp1ed18.415, csp2ed18.415, ms18Sn_1053, pts-vp-pli171, sya25.505, vnp875, vns882
